commands.repair_reason: - [P2] No repair lane is needed because the branch already contains the targeted implementation, regression tests, and sufficient proof with no remaining actionable findings.; Cleared: No security or supply-chain concern found; the PR changes local cache parsing, tests, a generated hash, and release-note text only. (sha=f9d54d1f5732fe97b7b222c2feedb69a2b675ea6)
commands.review_summary:
The PR memoizes models.dev cache load outcomes, invalidates the memo on save, updates the generated Codex parser hash, and adds regression tests for success, failure, and save-invalidation paths.

Reproducibility: yes. source inspection shows the fallback `modelsDevLookup` path calls `ModelsDevCache.load` when no pre-resolved catalog is available and scanner loops call cost lookup per row. I did not run the benchmark locally in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] No repair lane is needed because the branch already contains the targeted implementation, regression tests, and sufficient proof with no remaining actionable findings.

target.title: Memoize models.dev catalog to fix cost-scan CPU spike
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes a CPU spike / menu freeze during token-cost usage refreshes (reported in #1311).

When a cost lookup runs **without** a pre-resolved catalog, `CostUsagePricing.modelsDevLookup` falls back to `ModelsDevPricingPipeline.lookup` → `ModelsDevCache.load`, which reads and JSON-decodes the full **~800 KB** `models.dev` catalog from disk. That fallback is hit **once per usage row** (`codexModeSplitMaps` / Claude scan loops call `codexCostUSD` / `claudeCostUSD` per row). Whenever the top-level catalog load returns nil for a window — cold start, the 24 h TTL refresh writing the cache mid-scan, or a decode hiccup — the entire scan of a large `~/.codex` history re-decodes the catalog hundreds of thousands of times.

A macOS CPU resource report captured this precisely: **90 s of CPU at 95%** over a 94 s window, heaviest stack:

```
UsageStore.refreshTokenUsage
 └ CostUsageFetcher.loadTokenSnapshot
    └ CostUsageScanner.loadCodexDaily
       └ scanCodexFile → rescanCodexFile → codexModeSplitMaps
          └ CostUsagePricing.codexCostUSD
             └ ModelsDevCache.load
                └ JSONDecoder.decode   ← full ~800 KB catalog, per row
```

Because `refreshTokenUsage` runs when the menu is opened, this also presents as the menu **freezing on click**.

## Fix

Memoize the **load outcome** inside `ModelsDevCache`, keyed by the cache file's **path + size + modification date**:

- Repeated `load`s of an unchanged file reuse the in-memory result instead of re-reading/decoding, so the per-row fallback collapses to a stat + dictionary lookup.
- The whole outcome is cached, **including failures** — a corrupt or wrong-version cache is read and decode-attempted exactly as expensively as a valid one (a wrong-version file is fully decoded and only then rejected), so caching only successes would leave the per-row storm in place whenever the cache is unreadable.
- `isStale` still depends on `now`, so it is recomputed on every call; only the read+decode outcome is memoized.
- `save(...)` drops the memo entry; the size/mtime key also self-invalidates when the file changes underneath.
- Thread-safe via `NSLock` (cross-platform; the vendored CostUsage code also builds for Linux), mirroring the existing `PathEnvironment` / `ProviderStorageFootprint` cache helpers.

This does not change the threading of `modelsDevCatalog` through the scanner (which already exists); it just makes the nil-catalog fallback cheap so a transient nil can no longer turn a full history scan into a CPU storm.

The codex parser hash (which covers files under `Vendored/CostUsage`) is regenerated; this also invalidates stale cost-usage caches so they rebuild on the fixed path.

## Behavior proof (before/after)

Micro-benchmark of the hot path — repeated `ModelsDevCache.load` against a synthetic **2.1 MB** catalog (larger than the real ~788 KB, so conservative), 1000 iterations, debug build, same machine, `main` vs this branch:

| Cache state | `main` (unfixed) | this branch | speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| valid | **42.54 ms/load** | **0.108 ms/load** | ~394× |
| invalid-version | **42.69 ms/load** | **0.107 ms/load** | ~399× |

The `invalid-version` row demonstrates the failed-load path: on `main` it costs the same ~43 ms/load as a valid decode (full decode, then version rejection); with the fix it is memoized to ~0.1 ms/load. At hundreds of thousands of rows per refresh this is the difference between a multi-second main-thread stall and a single decode. (Benchmark was run with a temporary, uncommitted test; happy to share the harness.)

## Tests

Added to `ModelsDevPricingTests`:

- `serves decoded catalog from memo while the file is unchanged` — corrupts the cache bytes while preserving size + mtime; asserts the load still returns the originally decoded artifact (no re-decode).
- `serves a failed load from memo while the file is unchanged` — primes a failure, then restores valid bytes of identical size + mtime; asserts the cached failure is still returned (the now-valid file is not read/decoded again).
- `saving a new catalog invalidates the memo` — asserts a subsequent load reflects freshly written data.

## Commands executed

```
swift build                                       # Build complete
swift test --filter 'ModelsDevPricingTests|CostUsage'  # 185 passed
make check                                        # swiftformat --lint + swiftlint --strict → 0 violations
./Scripts/regenerate-codex-parser-hash.sh
```

No UI changes, so no screenshots.

Refs #1311
target.author: turbothad
